rss_0_1784921709.title: Two Russian men jailed in Angola for terrorism and spying
rss_0_1784921709.text:
Two Russian men have been found guilty by an Angolan court of terrorism and spying after being accused of planning a coup and helping to organise deadly demonstrations in the southern African country last year.

Lev Lakshtanov and Igor Ratchin were sentenced to eight and 11 years in jail respectively. They had denied the charges, claiming they were in Angola for cultural and business activities.

From 28-30 July 2025, a minibus taxi strike over fuel price hikes descended into riots and looting, in which 22 people were killed and more than 1,600 arrested according to official figures.

Angola’s security forces were accused of firing live ammunition at protesters; the government said its “progressive” use of force was justified. Human Rights Watch had previously said Angola employed “excessive force” when it used rubber bullets and teargas to break up a peaceful protest against rising fuel prices on 12 July 2025.

Angolan prosecutors said Lakshtanov and Ratchin wanted to influence next year’s presidential elections, including by funding Angola’s largest opposition party, the National Union for the Total Independence of Angola (UNITA), or by forcing a leadership change within the Popular Movement for the Liberation of Angola (MPLA), which has ruled the country since independence from Portugal in 1975.

Prosecutors also claimed the men were linked to Africa Politology, a Russian disinformation and propaganda network that emerged from the Wagner mercenary group.

Africa Politology was taken over by Russia’s Foreign Intelligence Service (SVR) after the death of the Wagner chief Yevgeny Prigozhin in 2023, according to an investigation by media outlets including Forbidden Stories. Wagner was rebranded as Africa Corps and absorbed by Russia’s military.

View image in fullscreen Wood is burned on the street near where people vandalised stores during the first day of the taxi strike in Luanda, Angola, in July 2025. Photograph: Ampe Rogerio/EPA

Angola, whose economy is highly dependent on oil exports, has historically been close with Russia. During the cold war years of Angola’s 1975-2002 civil war, the Soviet Union and Cuba supported the MPLA, while the US and apartheid South Africa backed its opponents, including UNITA. However, Angola’s president, João Lourenço, has improved relations with the US and Europe since taking power in 2017.

In the last decade, Russia has increased its presence in Africa. The Wagner group and then Africa Corps have provided security for the Central African Republic’s president, Faustin-Archange Touadéra, since 2017.

In 2021, Wagner forces arrived in Mali to support the military junta. Africa Corps also backs the military regimes in neighbouring Niger and Burkina Faso. Since 2023, Russian troops have also been deployed in Equatorial Guinea, Libya and Sudan, according to a US Congress report.

Madagascar’s president, Michael Randrianirina, has also boosted ties with Russia since taking power in a coup in October 2025. There are Russians in his presidential guard and a weekly programme about Madagascar-Russia relations was launched earlier this month on national television.

Two Angolans were also tried alongside the Russians. The sports journalist Carlos Tomé received a two-year suspended prison sentence. Francisco de Oliveira, the secretary-general of UNITA’s youth wing, was acquitted.

Prosecutors have appealed against those sentences, according to the Portuguese news agency Lusa. Lakshtanov and Ratchin have also appealed against their sentences, but will remain in detention while the appeal is processed, according to Lusa.

Russia’s embassy in Angola did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

Agence France-Press contributed to this report

rss_1_1784921709.title: MoD cancels army training in Kenya over powers to prosecute British soldiers
rss_1_1784921709.text:
The British army has been forced to cancel training for its troops in Kenya after the Kenyan government refused to ratify a bilateral defence agreement.

While the UK government wanted to renew the existing agreement, Kenyan lawmakers had sought changes to give local authorities greater powers to hold British soldiers accountable for any offences committed in the country.

According to the Kenyan news outlet the Daily Nation, the dispute centres on which cases Kenya will retain primary jurisdiction over, while the amount of money Britain pays Kenya to host the training camp has also been a sticking point.

The chair of the Kenyan national assembly’s defence, intelligence and foreign relations committee, Nelson Koech, said the two governments were “actively engaging to address these outstanding issues”.

As a result of the Kenyan government’s decision, a planned exercise involving the 1st Battalion the Duke of Lancaster’s Regiment, which was due to begin in September, has been cancelled.

A Ministry of Defence spokesperson said: “The UK highly values its relationship with Kenya, which is underpinned by mutual benefit, respect and cooperation. The UK-Kenya Defence Partnership is one of the great strengths of our relationship. Our joint training and operations with the Kenyan Defence Forces are keeping both Kenyan and British people safe.

“It is with regret that the necessary licences required to allow training to take place in Laikipia later this year have not been forthcoming. As a result, the planned training will now be conducted at an alternative location outside Kenya.”

Kenya also benefits from training of its own armed forces at BATUK, the British army base near Nanyuki, and the presence of British troops is valuable for the economy.

The MoD spokesperson added: “We recognise the disappointment this decision will cause for those who would have benefited from the economic activity generated by the exercise.”

They said the UK government remained “committed to our defence partnership with Kenya”, adding that the UK would “continue to engage closely with the Kenyan government to support the resumption of training in-country at the earliest appropriate opportunity”.

However, there has been sustained pressure from Kenyan people opposed to the British army’s continued presence. Last year, a report to the Kenyan parliament into the base alleged human rights violations, environmental destruction and sexual abuse by British soldiers.

The most high-profile case linked to the base is the death of Agnes Wanjiru, in which a British soldier has been charged with murder. Kenya has retained jurisdiction in the case and an extradition hearing is due to take place in November. Robert Purkiss denies the single charge of murder.

Legal proceedings had stalled since Wanjiru’s death in 2012, but advanced under John Healey’s tenure as defence secretary. As shadow defence secretary, he had called on the previous government to do more to progress the case.

Koech said the committee had called for “stronger accountability measures, including jurisdiction over serious offences, enhanced protection for local communities, and implementation of recommendations arising from the BATUK inquiry”.

rss_2_1784921710.title: Health worker monitored for Ebola in London hospital after working in DRC
rss_2_1784921710.text:
A humanitarian worker is being monitored at a specialist hospital in London after potential exposure to the Ebola virus.

The UK resident was medically evacuated from the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) where the worker had been treating patients with the disease, the UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) said.

UKHSA said the person was evacuated as a “highly precautionary measure”, adding: “The individual is not currently displaying any symptoms and remains well, but as a precautionary measure they have been evacuated on a flight chartered solely for the evacuation.

“They are now being assessed and monitored in isolation by infectious disease specialists at a London hospital.”

The agency said the incubation period of Ebola ranges from two to 21 days and the individual will be closely monitored and is required to isolate throughout this period.

There are no confirmed cases of Ebola in the UK and the risk to the general public remains low.

Richard Pebody, the director of epidemic and emerging infections at the UKHSA, said: “This individual has been transferred out of an abundance of caution and we’re pleased they remain well.”

Last month, a patient suspected of having the Ebola virus at a Glasgow hospital tested negative.

Precautionary measures were taken after the patient was admitted to the Queen Elizabeth University hospital.

An outbreak of the disease in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Uganda in May was declared a public health emergency of international concern by the World Health Organization (WHO).

The virus is transmitted to people from wild animals and then spreads in the human population through direct contact with the blood, secretions, organs or other bodily fluids of infected people and with contaminated surfaces and materials such as bedding or clothing.

According to WHO, symptoms can include fever, fatigue, malaise, muscle, pain, headache and sore throat. These are followed by vomiting, diarrhoea, abdominal pain, rash, and symptoms of impaired kidney and liver functions.

rss_3_1784921710.title: Healthy diet too expensive for one in three people globally, UN report finds
rss_3_1784921710.text:
One in three people cannot afford to eat a healthy diet, a new report has found, prompting calls for urgent action on food affordability.

There are 2.69 billion people unable to afford a diverse diet that meets their energy and nutrient needs, and it will take “immense” amounts of work to achieve the global target of zero hunger by 2030, the annual report from five UN agencies warned.

Global hunger declined for a third consecutive year in 2025, with 7.8% of people affected, down from 8.1% in 2024. There were also signs that a prolonged upward trend in Africa was coming to a halt.

However, the proportion of people who cannot afford a healthy diet remains “extremely high”, said Maximo Torero, chief economist of the Food and Agriculture Organization, one of the report’s authors.

The State of Food Security and Nutrition in the World 2026 report calculated the cost of a healthy diet is 4.28 purchasing power parity (PPP) dollars a day. This means that in any given country, a healthy diet costs the amount of local currency with the same buying power that $4.28 has in the US.

“The extreme poverty line is $3PPP per person,” Torero said. “So it’s significantly higher.”

View image in fullscreen A typical Moroccan dish – Africa is now home to the highest number of hungry people for the first time, with about 309 million. Photograph: Crevous/Alamy

He said government policies had typically focused on subsidising cereals and starchy staples, rather than meat, dairy, fruit and vegetables.

“That means calories, but not a diversity of diet,” he said, highlighting the impact of this on rates of obesity and diseases such as cancer and diabetes.

Figures from the World Health Organization, another of the UN agencies behind the report, show adult obesity rose from 12.1% in 2012 to 16.2% in 2024.

“It’s extremely urgent,” Torero said. “Obesity [among adults] is growing at a very steep level, so we need to now work intensively on trying to minimise those risks and those hidden costs that that substantial increase in obesity will bring.”

He called for investment in infrastructure, research and development. Modelling in the report suggests more efficient supply chains could bring down the costs of meat and dairy as well as fruit and vegetables. New policies to encourage greater consumption of fruit and vegetables could also pay dividends, he suggested, reducing the economic impact of health issues linked to poor diets.

In Africa, 66.6% of the population was unable to afford a healthy diet in 2025, more than double the levels observed in Asia or Latin America and the Caribbean.

Africa is now home to the highest number of hungry people for the first time, with about 309 million, surpassing Asia’s 292 million, the report showed.

The rise is largely driven by higher population growth on the continent, Torero said, with the proportion of the population going hungry actually starting to decrease “for the first time in years”.

“I think this is extremely important, because the numbers are for 2025, where already we have seen a significant reduction in overseas development assistance in the region,” Torero said.

Shipping restrictions in the strait of Hormuz linked to the conflict in the Middle East are likely to hit next year’s figures, he said.

The report is authored by the FAO, WHO, Unicef, the UN World Food Programme and the International Fund for Agricultural Development.

rss_4_1784921710.title: São Tomé and Príncipe heads to polls in tense presidential election
rss_4_1784921710.text:
Voters in São Tomé and Príncipe go to the polls for a presidential election on Sunday as one of Africa’s least populous countries seeks to burnish its democratic credentials.

According to the National Election Commission, about 142,000 people are registered to vote in the tiny African state’s elections, approximately 15% of whom live in the diaspora.

Since gaining independence from Portugal in 1975, the island country off Africa’s west coast has built a solid reputation for peaceful, competitive elections. But this year’s vote comes amid a tense political atmosphere and a lingering constitutional crisis.

The president, Carlos Vila Nova, is running for a second term as an independent, rather than as the candidate for the ruling Acção Democrática Independente (ADI), whom he represented when he was elected in 2021.

The tensions between Vila Nova and his former party began in January last year when he dismissed the prime minister, Patrice Trovoada. Trovoada’s replacement, the former justice minister Ilza Amado Vaz, resigned after just three days, before the current holder, Américo Ramos, took office.

On Sunday, Vila Nova will be running against four other candidates including the ADI parliamentary leader, Nito D’Abreu. Another former prime minister, Jorge Bom Jesus, attempted to withdraw his independent candidacy, but missed the deadline and remains on the ballot.

The main opposition party, the Movement for the Liberation of São Tomé and Príncipe, is now part of a broad coalition backing the incumbent president, despite being staunch ADI opponents. Meanwhile, an ADI faction led by Ramos is backing D’Abreu. If no candidate receives more than 50% of the vote, the election will go on to a second round.

View image in fullscreen A view across the island of Príncipe. Photograph: Constantino Leite/Alamy

The businessman Domingos Monteiro, known as “Nino”, who has been president of the São Toméan Football Federation since 2015, was disqualified after the constitutional court ruled he did not meet the eligibility requirements. Monteiro was born on the island, but his parents never became naturalised citizens after moving from Cape Verde where they were born.

“The constitutional court is violating the principle of equal rights … It is time for descendants born in São Tomé and Príncipe to say no to discrimination, to the culture of xenophobia and persecution,” said Monteiro.

For voters, priorities include tackling government corruption, high inflation, severe youth unemployment, chronic fuel shortages, and frequent blackouts. Rivals of Vila Nova are hoping this will keep him from a second term and propel them to the presidency.

Corruption in São Tomé and Príncipe “is the greatest catastrophe the country has experienced in this half-century [of independence”, D’Abreu said at the presidential debate on Tuesday.

The country is seen as a trusted western ally for security and maritime trade in the Gulf of Guinea, one of the world’s biggest maritime piracy hotspots. It also has offshore oil deposits that make a significant contribution to its economy. Observer missions from the European Union, G7+ and the Community of Portuguese Language Countries are on the ground to monitor the polls.

rss_5_1784921710.title: Thousands of lightning strikes trigger fresh wildfires in western Canada
rss_5_1784921710.text:
Fresh wildfires have broken out in western Canada and forests continued to ⁠burn in Ontario as the country ⁠battled what the prime ​minister, Mark Carney, called one of the most intense wildfire seasons on record.

Nearly 34,000 lightning strikes over the past week led to a series of fires ⁠and evacuation orders in southern British Columbia. There were 25 evacuation orders and 90 active wildfires in the province as of Friday morning, and authorities said warming ⁠and drying conditions could cause them to grow.

Hugh Murdoch, the incident commander of a cluster of fires near Kimberley, 320 miles (520km) from Vancouver, said: “The weather forecast is far from favourable. There is no significant ​help from mother nature whatsoever in the foreseeable ‌future.”

Wildfires fuelled by intense heat and ‌lightning storms attributed to warmer temperatures have burned remote forests across Canada this summer. Smoke from them drifted across large US east coast and midwestern cities last week.

View image in fullscreen Smoke from Canadian wildfire smoke hanging in the air over New York last week. Photograph: Christopher Neundorf/EPA

There were 139 active wildfires in north-west Ontario, of which 32 were not under control.

Environment Canada issued an alert for poor air quality in Banff, Alberta and surrounding areas on Friday but said conditions were expected to improve for most areas over the weekend.

Carney ‌said in a statement that the federal government would mobilise more than 5,300 firefighters and about 300 water bombers, helicopters and evacuation aircraft to battle the fires. Mexico would also send as many as 200 firefighters in the coming weeks, he added.

Thousands of people, including some First Nations communities, have been evacuated this summer. About 2.95m hectares (7.29m acres) of land have been burned this year, above the 10-year ⁠average of about 2.55m hectares. Canada currently has more active wildfires than it did at the same ​time in either of the ​past two years.

The country’s forested areas account ​for 28% of the world’s boreal forests.

Improving weather in the east has helped Quebec tame ​some blazes with help ‌from firefighters from ​neighbouring provinces, France and the ​US.

Stéphane Caron,, of the province’s forest fire prevention agency Sopfeu, said: “The situation in Quebec is under control. We have good weather to help us. We have to make sure that there’s no hotspot on the ground any more because if there’s hot weather and no rain, the fire can be back.”

rss_6_1784921710.title: Guyanese authorities confirm death of 72 people in sinking of coastal ferry
rss_6_1784921710.text:
The confirmed death toll from the recent capsizing of a state ferry off Guyana’s coast has risen to 72, with more bodies recovered as a multinational team struggle to salvage the partially submerged vessel.

Of the estimated 179 people aboard, 76 people were rescued. The government has estimated that 100 may have died in the accident, which would make it the worst marine tragedy on record for the South American nation.

The prime minister, Mark Phillips, said late on Thursday that crews have widened the search area and are determined to find all those who were aboard so loved ones can bury them with dignity.

“The government of Guyana continues to stand in solidarity with all those affected during this difficult time and will ensure that every available resource is deployed until the operation is brought to a safe and dignified conclusion,” he said.

The 87-year-old MV Barima departed the capital, Georgetown, on Saturday and was headed to Port Kaituma when it began taking on water and capsized in Atlantic waters off the Essequibo coast.

Phillips said the ferry was not insured, and the government is paying for the funerals and other related expenses.

Divers from countries including Brazil and Trinidad and Tobago are trying to salvage the vessel, with officials saying they hope to inspect it for any possible damage that could shed light on what happened.

Police have launched a criminal investigation and detained three people so far, including the rescued captain and a crew member the authorities say tested positive for marijuana.

Also detained is the marine superintendent responsible for overseeing the loading of the ferry. The government maintains it was under capacity in both people and cargo, but survivors have told reporters that they believe the vessel was overloaded.

The ship’s manifest listed 116 passengers and 17 crew, but authorities believe as many as 179 people were aboard.

A growing number of Guyanese are questioning why it took so long to rescue people, with some survivors telling the Associated Press that they spent hours in the water.

Phillips has asked for patience and said officials are awaiting the conclusion of an investigation. Its findings will be reviewed by a commission of inquiry, which has not yet been established.

Asked why an 87-year-old ferry was still in service, the government said it had acquired a new vessel from India in recent months to replace the MV Barima, but was waiting on the completion of a new pier at Port Kaituma needed to accommodate it.

The MV Barima was last dry docked in 2024 for major repairs and was scheduled for additional work in October.

rss_7_1784921711.title: Canadian government urged to block Thomson Reuters data deal with US ICE
rss_7_1784921711.text:
A Canadian federal opposition party leader has called on Mark Carney’s government to block the latest data-sharing deal between US Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and the Canadian media company Thomson Reuters.

Avi Lewis, the federal leader of the New Democratic party (NDP), demanded on Tuesday that Ottawa address the contract and further restrict Canadian corporations from doing business with ICE, after Canada’s National Observer news website reported on the deal.

“My blood ran cold when I read about this development,” he added in a statement to the Guardian. “Complicity between Canadian corporations and ICE should absolutely be illegal.”

Under the US$125m (£94m) deal, ICE will pay the technology and content conglomerate to access its Clear database – which includes property records, social media and geolocation information – in order to identify “voter fraud” and “immigration fraud”, according to reports.

In a procurement document obtained by 404 Media, Thomson Reuters says it is the only company that allows ICE to have access to the “continuous monitoring” of millions of people.

Thomson Reuters’ contracts with ICE date back to 2015, when it first began providing US officials with access to Clear in order to track people for deportation. Critics have long alleged that the databases were being used to facilitate human rights abuses.

The latest contract, however, is the first to state that Clear software will be used to support the Trump administration’s objectives of addressing supposed “voter fraud”.

In a recent primetime television address, the president repeated false claims about the supposed fallibility of the 2020 US presidential election results, alleging voter fraud, noncitizen voting and a host of other misleading or incorrect statements. Election officials from across the political spectrum lambasted the speech and reasserted the reliability of US elections.

Thomson Reuters is owned by the Woodbridge Company, a holding company for the Thomson family, frequently described as the richest family in Canada. The media company also owns the news agency Reuters and the Globe and Mail newspaper.

A spokesperson for Thomson Reuters said it did not comment on customer contracts, and referred to a page on the company’s website about Clear.

The post says there are “misunderstandings” about the tool, and that it is licensed “exclusively to select businesses, law enforcement and government agencies to help expedite investigative processes in legitimate legal investigations”.

The company emphasises that its “investigative solutions are not surveillance tools”.

Lewis said the federal government should deny export permits to companies selling equipment to ICE, and pull public subsidies or contracts from any companies that have dealings with the agency. In January, the NDP made similar calls after the fatal shootings of two US citizens by ICE in Minneapolis.

“If the Carney government is serious about Canadian economic independence, it should move to block this contract,” Lewis said.

The prime minister’s office did not immediately respond to a request for comment on the Thomson Reuters contract.

Get in touch Contact us about this story The best public interest journalism relies on first-hand accounts from people in the know. If you have something to share on this subject, you can contact us confidentially using the following methods: Secure Messaging in the Guardian app The Guardian app has a tool to send tips about stories. Messages are end to end encrypted and concealed within the routine activity that every Guardian mobile app performs. This prevents an observer from knowing that you are communicating with us at all, let alone what is being said. If you don’t already have the Guardian app, download it (iOS/Android) and go to the menu. Select ‘Secure Messaging’. SecureDrop If you can safely use the tor network without being observed or monitored you can send messages and documents to the Guardian via our SecureDrop platform. Our guide at theguardian.com/tips lists several ways to contact us securely, and discusses the pros and cons of each. Show more

Joel Bakan, a law professor at the University of British Columbia and author of several books on the unethical behaviour of corporations, said the legal framework did exist in Canada to bar companies from working with ICE.

“Companies are not allowed to engage with various rogue regimes, authoritarian regimes, and certainly enemy regimes,” he said. “It’s definitely within the jurisdiction of the government, including the federal government of Canada, to impose various kinds of limitations.”

He pointed to Canada’s Special Economic Measures Act, the country’s primary sanctions law, which can authorise the government to restrict trade in relation to human rights violations. “As a matter of realpolitik, I can’t imagine the federal government invoking this against the US, but it is at least arguable that ICE has been involved in such violations,” he said.

rss_8_1784921711.title: Masked men invading and taking Amazon communities ‘hostage’ in organised crime surge, say local leaders
rss_8_1784921711.text:
Indigenous leaders on the Brazilian-Peruvian border have said their communities are being “taken hostage” by organised crime groups who are running rampant across the region, destroying rainforest, building roads and trafficking drugs.

Earlier this month, masked men armed with semi-automatic weapons entered homes in Apiwtxa, a village in the Kampa do Rio Amônia Indigenous territory, intimidating people and searching for targets.

The Asháninka people, who have faced threats from intruders for decades because of their efforts to protect the forest and eject narco smugglers, say this is the worst danger they have faced, prompting an emergency delegation to Brasília this week to appeal for support from the government of the president, Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva.

“Either the state steps in to provide protection, or communities will start organising to defend themselves – and that would be disastrous,” said Francisco Piyãko, the general coordinator of the Organisation of Indigenous People of the Juruá River, who is part of the delegation. “We are not facing a local conflict. We are facing the expansion of transnational criminal networks into Indigenous territories that currently represent one of the last barriers against the illegal occupation of this region of the Amazon.”

View image in fullscreen The Asháninka Indigenous people are seeking protection from the Brazilian government. Photograph: Jorge Sáenz/AP

The petition of the Asháninka people comes amid growing concerns about the nexus of drug trafficking and environmental crime, which Brazilian climate scientist Carlos Nobre has described as the greatest new threat to the Amazon Forest.

The Brazilian side of the border is in Acre state, where the forest is still relatively healthy compared with the Peruvian side, which has experienced heavy logging and violence. Acre has sometimes been a perilous area for environmental and land defenders. One of the country’s most celebrated forest campaigners, Chico Mendes, was shot dead there in 1988. After that, there was a period of relative peace in Acre, compared with other countries in the Amazon or other Brazilian states such as Pará. More recently, however, there has been a rise in tension, much of it from the outside. Isolated Indigenous communities from Peru have been increasingly crossing the border to escape confrontation with loggers and drug cartels.

Apiwtxa was the last Indigenous village visited by the British journalist Dom Phillips before he was murdered in the neighbouring Brazilian border state of Amazonas in 2022 while researching illegal extractivist activities in another area menaced by arms and drug smugglers.

View image in fullscreen Sunrise on the Amônia River, which runs through the Kampa do Rio Amônia Indigenous territory in Brazil. Photograph: Nicoló Lanfranchi/The Guardian

According to the Asháninka petition to the Brazilian government, the most recent intrusion into Apiwtxa involved five heavily armed, Spanish-speaking men arriving on the evening of 6 July at the home of one of the Asháninka leaders, who was out of the village at the time. “They were clearly poised to attack with lethal intent,” said one member of the community. It was the second time in several days that masked gunmen had entered the village looking for leaders.

Frightened villagers took their children into one building for protection and notified the authorities. The army arrived at midnight, by which time the would-be assassins had disappeared back into the forest, leaving only tracks.

“For us, this was a threat unlike any other, because they came right into our home – they entered our house,” said Piyãko. “We realise that the context we’re living in now isn’t just about drug trafficking; you can see it’s a movement involving everything from wildcat mining and illegal mining to illegal logging, drug trafficking and other issues.”

View image in fullscreen Francisco Piyãko, general coordinator of the Organisation of Indigenous People of the Juruá River, is part of the delegation to Brasília. Photograph: Martín Mejía/AP

The Asháninka delegation in Brasília is calling for a permanent security presence along the border to prevent the free movement of drug traffickers, illegal loggers and the illicit construction of “virus roads” that push into the forest without environmental oversight. They also want greater protection for their leaders and operations to combat organised crime in the area.

The neighbouring Peruvian department of Ucayali has the widest network of illegal roads in the Peruvian Amazon, providing transport for gangs involved in environmental crime, gold, money laundering and drugs. In the past five years, coca cultivation in Ucayali has tripled.

The Igarapé Institute, a public security thinktank, has said that organised crime – which often intersects with narcotics and illegal extraction of natural resources – needs a more robust response by governments across the region: “It is increasingly clear that the fight against organised environmental crime in the Amazon ultimately demands better intelligence and strategic prioritisation. The goal must be to strengthen Brazilian state authority to deter and repress crimes and those who commit them.”

Piyãko said this was now a matter of urgency. He added: “If the Peruvian and Brazilian governments joined forces and wanted to resolve the situation, they could. Because they have the means to combat this.”

“If the state doesn’t protect these territories, they will be dominated by this criminal system, or the people will be forced to defend themselves on their own. This could lead to very serious situations, because as the net tightens around these territories, tensions rise.”

rss_9_1784921711.title: Weather tracker: More than 900 wildfires rage across Canada
rss_9_1784921711.text:
Recent hot, dry weather in Canada has triggered hundreds of wildfires across the country. As of Wednesday, more than 900 were burning, scorching about 3m hectares of land. While fires have broken out in several provinces, the greatest concern is in the Northwest Territories and Ontario, where several remain out of control.

Poor air quality has been reported across affected regions, with wildfire smoke carried by winds into the US and even across the Atlantic to northern Europe. The risk of further blazes remains high as North America enters the peak of fire season.

Meanwhile, the city of Salta in Argentina recorded its warmest-ever overnight minimum temperature on Saturday, with the mercury failing to fall below 26C (79F). This reading is particularly remarkable because Argentina is in the middle of winter, when Salta’s average night-time minimum is about 3C.

The previous warmest night at this weather station was 22.2C, recorded in January 1953 during the height of summer. This exceptional warmth was driven by the Zonda wind, which develops when westerly airflow crosses the Andes before descending, warming and drying as it sinks back to ground level.

This process is known as the Foehn effect, a phenomenon that occurs on the downwind side of mountain ranges around the world. However, the intensity experienced in Salta last weekend was unusual. One contributing factor may have been the rapidly developing El Niño in the eastern Pacific, which would have left the incoming air mass warmer than average, even before it descended on the city.

Elsewhere, the strengthening El Niño is also expected to increase typhoon activity in the western Pacific. Tropical Depression 11 formed on Tuesday before developing into Tropical Storm Noul north of the Philippines on Thursday night. The system is forecast to intensify into a typhoon over the coming days as it tracks between the Philippines and Taiwan, before making landfall on China’s south-east coast on Sunday morning local time as a category 1 storm.
